perturbation.original_text: Chemsultants agrees to (a) keep all Confidential Information confidential and to not, at any time during or after any transaction where Confidential Information disclosures are made, publish, disclose, or divulge Confidential Information to any person, firm, or corporation other than its Representatives and to cause its Representatives to observe the terms of this Agreement; (b) not use, directly or indirectly, copy, or reproduce any Confidential Information of Client for its own benefit or for the benefit of any person, firm, or corporation other than Client, and in any event only in connection with evaluating or engaging in a transaction or business relationship with Client.
perturbation.changed_text: Chemsultants will endeavor to (a) keep Confidential Information confidential and to attempt to not, publish, disclose, or divulge Confidential Information to any person, firm, or corporation other than its Representatives and to encourage its Representatives to observe the terms of this Agreement; (b) not use, directly or indirectly, copy, or reproduce any Confidential Information of Client for its own benefit or for the benefit of any person, firm, or corporation other than Client, and in any event only in connection with evaluating or engaging in a transaction or business relationship with Client.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to' to 'will endeavor to' and 'cause its Representatives to' to 'encourage its Representatives to' weakens the obligation, making it a goal rather than a requirement. The wording becomes ambiguous and less enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1333.61 provides a civil action for misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to keep confidential information confidential, the modified text may make it more difficult for Client to prove misappropriation under Ohio law, as Chemsultants is only agreeing to 'endeavor' to maintain confidentiality and 'encourage' its representatives, rather than strictly ensuring it.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1333.61 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1333.61 | Uniform trade secrets act definitions. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1333 Trade Practices Effective: July 20, 1994 Latest Legislation: House Bill 320 - 120th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF As used in sections 1333.61 to 1333.69 of the Revised Code, unless the context requires otherwise: (A) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (B) "Misappropriation" means any of the following: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without the express or implied consent of the other person by a person who did any of the following: (a) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (b) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the knowledge of the trade secret that the person acquired was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or was derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (c) Before a material change of their position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (C) "Person" has the same meaning as in division (C) of section 1.59 of the Revised Code and includes governmental entities. (D) "Trade
perturbation.justification: The change from "agrees to" to "will endeavor to" and from "cause its Representatives to" to "encourage its Representatives to" weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. The law snippet defines "Improper means" which includes "breach of a duty to maintain secrecy". By weakening the obligation, the changed text makes it more difficult to prove a breach of duty, thus creating a contradiction. The original text created a clear obligation which the modified text undermines.

perturbation.original_text: Upon written request by Client at any time, Chemsultants shall promptly return all Confidential Information, including all copies, reproductions, summaries, analyses or extracts thereof or based thereon in the party’s p o s s e s s i o n o r i n t h e p o s s e s s i o n o f a n y R e p r e s e n t a t i v e o f t h e p a r t y , p r o v i d e d , however, Chemsultants shall be permitted to charge Client for all reasonable costs incurred by Chemsultants for compliance with this Section.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon written request by Client, Chemsultants will return what it deems to be the essential Confidential Information in its direct possession, provided Chemsultants believes it is reasonable to do so. Chemsultants may charge Client for costs associated with this return.
perturbation.explanation: The terms 'what it deems to be essential Confidential Information', 'in its direct possession', and 'believes it is reasonable to do so' all introduce ambiguity. Chemsultants now has discretion to decide what information to return, whether that information is essential, and whether returning the data is reasonable, undermining the Client's right to retrieve all confidential information. Removing 'at any time' can be a legal issue, as the text limits the Client's power.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1333.61 protects trade secrets. By allowing Chemsultants to decide what confidential information to return, the modification weakens Client's ability to protect its trade secrets and potentially recover misappropriated information under Ohio law.

(B) "Misappropriation" means any of the following: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without the express or implied consent of the other person by a person who did any of the following: (a) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (b) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the knowledge of the trade secret that the person acquired was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or was derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (c) Before a material change of their position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity that weakens the client's ability to protect and recover their trade secrets, potentially contradicting Ohio Revised Code § 1333.61. The original text mandates the return of 'all Confidential Information', while the changed text allows Chemsultants to return 'what it deems to be the essential Confidential Information', giving Chemsultants discretion. The scraped snippet defines 'Misappropriation' in 1333.61(B)(2) as the 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without the express or implied consent of the other person'. By limiting what is returned, Chemsultants could potentially retain information that could be used, disclosed, or acquired by improper means. This alteration undermines the client's right to maintain secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, construed, and enforced in accordance with the laws, and in the courts, of the State of Ohio, County of Lake, without giving effect to any conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be generally governed by, construed, and enforced with reference to the laws of the State of Ohio.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'and in the courts, of the State of Ohio, County of Lake' eliminates the specified jurisdiction for dispute resolution, and removing "without giving effect to any conflict of laws principles." allows for the consideration of conflicts of law, potentially leading to uncertainty about which jurisdiction's laws apply. Changing 'in accordance with' to 'with reference to' weakens the binding nature.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 2307.39 pertains to contract choice of law and forum selection provisions. By removing the specific forum selection (County of Lake), the modified text opens the door for disputes about jurisdiction, potentially contradicting the intent of Ohio law to enforce agreements on choice of forum. The law aims to avoid uncertainty and litigation over where a lawsuit may be commenced. The change injects uncertainty which goes against the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 2307.39 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 2307.39 | Agreements to be bound by Ohio law. Ohio Revised Code / Title 23 Courts-Common Pleas / Chapter 2307 Civil Actions Effective: June 29, 2011 Latest Legislation: House Bill 9 - 129th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) Except as provided in division (C) of this section, any person may bring a civil action in a court of this state against an individual, corporation, or other person who is a resident of, incorporated under the laws of, or otherwise engaged in the conduct of business in a foreign nation or a province, territory, or other political subdivision of a foreign nation, against a foreign nation, or against a province, territory, or other political subdivision of a foreign nation upon a cause of action that arises out of or relates to a contingent or other contract, agreement, or undertaking, whether or not it bears a reasonable relation to this state, if the contract, agreement, or undertaking contains both of the following provisions: (1) An agreement by the parties to be governed in their rights and duties under the contract, agreement, or undertaking, in whole or in part, by the law of this state; (2) An agreement by the parties to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of this state. (B) The court shall not stay or dismiss a civil action brought in accordance with division (A) of this section on the ground of inconvenient forum. In the civil action, the court shall apply the law of this state as agreed upon by the parties. (C) This section applies to a transaction covered by section 1301.301 of the Revised Code unless the transaction is subject to a limitation on choice of law specified in division (B) of that sect
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies the jurisdiction (County of Lake) and excludes conflict of laws principles. The changed text removes both these stipulations, creating ambiguity about where disputes will be resolved and which laws will apply. This contradicts the intent of Ohio Revised Code § 2307.39, which, as described in the Law Explanation, aims to enforce agreements on choice of forum and avoid uncertainty. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 2307.39 (A)(2) states that a contract may contain "(2) An agreement by the parties to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of this state." By removing the specific forum, the changed text undermines the ability to enforce this agreement, thus creating a contradiction.
